remaining_tasks.name: Valkey-style IO architecture rework
remaining_tasks.description: Replace per-worker mio::Poll with consolidated single-poll on apply thread; IO workers become pure SPSC/SPMC consumers (Valkey IOThreadMain pattern). Plan doc has 4-phase migration (A measure → B consolidate → C maxclients/backpressure → D validate). Phase A is the gate — measure cross-thread channel overhead before committing to rework. If <5% of total push CPU, abandon rework.

remaining_tasks.name: Investigate per-entity memory overhead — fraud-team measured at ~22 KB/entity vs CLAUDE.md budget of 7 KB
remaining_tasks.description:
Empirical per-entity overhead from r8g maxcard test:
 - small (1 count feature): ~1 KB/entity
 - medium (5 features count/sum/avg/min/max): ~5.6 KB/entity
 - large_phase9 (15 features incl. decay/velocity): ~18 KB/entity
 - fraud-team (~90 features × 9 aggs): ~22 KB/entity
Likely culprits: HLL state (count_distinct), UDDSketch (percentile), TopK SpaceSaving (top_k), histogram bounded buffers. CLAUDE.md 7KB budget assumed a 'rich 30-feature pack' — the heavier sketch ops carry significantly more state. Either (a) cut overhead 3× before claiming 1M-entity-per-instance for fraud-team-shape; (b) reframe v0 pitch as '1M entities per simple-shape feature pack'; (c) recommend r8g.8xlarge (256 GiB) for fraud-team workloads.

remaining_tasks.name: Commit beava-bench-v2 + Cargo.toml + valkey plan doc via /gsd-quick
remaining_tasks.description: Three uncommitted files on v2/greenfield: crates/beava-bench/src/bin/beava-bench-v2.rs (~890 LOC), crates/beava-bench/Cargo.toml (added [[bin]] entry), .planning/ideas/valkey-io-architecture-rework.md

decisions.decision: beava-bench-v2 written from scratch instead of patching beava-bench-v18
decisions.rationale: v18's pre-built pool model (Vec<Bytes> of 1024 frames cycled forever) makes large-cardinality tests impossible; v2 generates payloads on the fly so cardinality is unbounded by RAM. Also added multi-target sharding, pattern sweep, built-in /metrics scraper, hash-derived extra fields (so per-aggregation cardinality is bounded by user_id cardinality not by push count).

decisions.decision: PID-file restart pattern for bench cycle (vs pkill -f /work/beava)
decisions.rationale: Discovered mid-session: pkill -f matches its own bash script's cmdline (which contains the path), self-killing before completing. PID-file pattern (write $! at startup, kill -9 $(cat pidfile) at restart) is bulletproof. Implemented in /tmp/beava-bench-aws/restart-helper.sh.

decisions.decision: Confirmed beava IO architecture diverges from Valkey
decisions.rationale: Code comments in io_thread_worker.rs claim 'Valkey 8 model' but Valkey IO threads are pure SPSC/SPMC consumers (verified from valkey-io/valkey/src/io_threads.c::IOThreadMain). Beava's IO workers each have their own mio::Poll and independently poll their assigned client subsets, sending parsed RingItems via crossbeam channel. This is N+1 epoll instances vs Valkey's 1.

decisions.decision: Per-stage trace confirms apply-CPU is the bottleneck (88%), NOT I/O
decisions.rationale: 5000 samples per arch: agg stage = 6555 ns (r8g) vs 3430 ns (M4); wal_append = 96 ns vs 51 ns; TOTAL = 7466 vs 4060. Memory bandwidth + L1/L2 cache hierarchy are the differentiator between Apple M4 and Graviton 4 Neoverse-V2 — not network/disk I/O.

decisions.decision: fraud-team per-entity overhead is 3× the CLAUDE.md budget
decisions.rationale: Empirical max-card test: 4M entities × ~22 KB = 90 GB RSS. CLAUDE.md budget says 7 KB/entity for 'rich 30-feature pack'. fraud-team has ~90 features × 9 aggs which is heavier than the budget assumption. Phase 13 must address this before claiming 1M entities/node.

next_action: Run /gsd-resume-work next session. Two follow-up tracks identified: (1) Valkey IO rework — read .planning/ideas/valkey-io-architecture-rework.md, decide whether to promote to a v0.1+ phase; (2) Per-entity memory overhead — start by running a microbench that measures size_of each AggOp variant + grep for the heaviest sketches (HLL state, UDDSketch buckets, TopK SpaceSaving counters, histogram bounded buffers).
